questions.question: What fundamental ML engineering problem does a Feature Store primarily eliminate?
options: The cost of training large language models on GPU clusters.

Feature duplication across ML teams and Train/Serve Skew — where different teams independently re-implement the same feature with subtle differences, and the feature computation used during model training is inconsistent with the computation used during real-time serving.
The need to tune model hyperparameters during training.
The latency of PostgreSQL join operations during batch training.
questions.answer: Feature duplication across ML teams and Train/Serve Skew — where different teams independently re-implement the same feature with subtle differences, and the feature computation used during model training is inconsistent with the computation used during real-time serving.
questions.explanation: The Feature Store is the single source of truth for all feature logic. Both training and serving call the same function, guaranteeing identical results.

questions.question: What is 'Train/Serve Skew' and why is it considered one of the most dangerous silent failures in production ML systems?
options: When the training dataset is larger than the serving dataset.

When the feature computation logic used during offline model training is subtly different from the feature computation logic used during real-time inference, causing the model to be evaluated on data that looks different from what it was trained on, silently degrading its performance.
When training takes longer than the deployment SLA.
When model weights exceed 4GB and can't be loaded into memory.
questions.answer: When the feature computation logic used during offline model training is subtly different from the feature computation logic used during real-time inference, causing the model to be evaluated on data that looks different from what it was trained on, silently degrading its performance.
questions.explanation: A fraud model trained with '30-day rolling average' using a specific SQL window function, but served with a different Python implementation that uses a slightly different window, will consistently underperform.

questions.question: A Feature Store maintains both an 'Offline Store' and an 'Online Store'. What is the key architectural difference between these two components?
options: The Offline Store uses Python; the Online Store uses Java.

The Offline Store is a Data Warehouse (like Snowflake) used for batch training, providing large-scale historical point-in-time feature retrieval. The Online Store is a low-latency key-value database (like Redis) used for real-time inference, providing millisecond feature lookups for live requests.
The Offline Store is encrypted; the Online Store is not.
The Offline Store holds raw data; the Online Store holds final model weights.
questions.answer: The Offline Store is a Data Warehouse (like Snowflake) used for batch training, providing large-scale historical point-in-time feature retrieval. The Online Store is a low-latency key-value database (like Redis) used for real-time inference, providing millisecond feature lookups for live requests.
questions.explanation: Training needs petabytes of historical data with complex joins. Serving needs the current feature value for a single user in under 10ms. These are two completely different performance requirements.
